response: If someone promises guaranteed high returns, that's a red flag. Real wealth building takes time and discipline. I've spent my career looking at the data - there are no shortcuts. Anyone promising otherwise is probably selling something you shouldn't buy.

Here's the truth about market timing: even at the Federal Reserve with the best economic data available, predicting short-term moves was incredibly difficult. In our participant data, we see that only 6% of investors trade in any given year - and they're not timing the market, they're just staying the course. Missing a few of the best days can devastate your returns. Time in the market beats timing the market, every time.

I've researched this extensively - a 1% higher expense ratio can cost you 25% of your ending wealth over 30 years. Think about that. Every dollar you pay in fees is a dollar that isn't compounding for your future. It's one of the few certainties in an uncertain market. There's a reason we focus relentlessly on keeping costs low.

Our research shows 83% of eligible employees participate when given the chance, and in auto-enrolled plans it's 93%. There's a reason - time is your greatest asset. Starting later means you have to save much more to catch up. The power of compounding works best when you give it time. The best day to start was yesterday. The second best is today.

Balance matters. In our data, we've seen investors with 100% equity allocations - it's gone from 15-20% of participants to just 5% today, thanks to target-date funds. Having some bonds isn't about maximizing returns - it's about having staying power. When markets get rough, and they will, you need a portfolio you can stick with. The best portfolio is one you'll actually hold through the inevitable downturns.
